comments.body: What makes you think that StackMapTable and StackDependencies are equivalent? Because there’s “stack” in the name?
That's exactly it. I was trying to remove custom code since implementing Javassist into the project.

answers.body:
Unfortunately, Javassist is terribly documented, and there are very limited resources that explain how to use it.

If you look at the source code for Walker, you'll see that several methods have empty bodies. This is because the intended usage of Walker is to create a subclass and override those various methods.

import javassist.bytecode.BadBytecode;
import javassist.bytecode.StackMapTable;
import javassist.bytecode.StackMapTable.Walker;

public class MyStackMapTableWalker extends Walker {

    public MyStackMapTableWalker(StackMapTable smt) {
        super(smt);
    }

    public MyStackMapTableWalker(byte[] data) {
        super(data);
    }

    @Override
    public void appendFrame(int pos, int offsetDelta, int[] tags, int[] data) throws BadBytecode {
        System.out.printf("appendFrame - {pos: %d, offsetDelta: %d}%n", pos, offsetDelta);
    }

    @Override
    public void chopFrame(int pos, int offsetDelta, int k) throws BadBytecode {
        System.out.printf("chopFrame - {pos: %d, offsetDelta: %d}%n", pos, offsetDelta);
    }

    @Override
    public void fullFrame(int pos, int offsetDelta, int[] localTags, int[] localData, int[] stackTags, int[] stackData)
            throws BadBytecode {
        System.out.printf("fullFrame - {pos: %d, offsetDelta: %d}%n", pos, offsetDelta);
    }

    @Override
    public void objectOrUninitialized(int tag, int data, int pos) {
        System.out.printf("objectOrUninitialized - {pos: %d}%n", pos);
    }

    @Override
    public void sameFrame(int pos, int offsetDelta) throws BadBytecode {
        System.out.printf("sameFrame - {pos: %d, offsetDelta: %d}%n", pos, offsetDelta);
    }

    @Override
    public void sameLocals(int pos, int offsetDelta, int stackTag, int stackData) throws BadBytecode {
        System.out.printf("sameLocals - {pos: %d, offsetDelta: %d}%n", pos, offsetDelta);
    }

}

Once you have done this, you call parse() and it will walk the stack map table, calling the appropriate methods as it visits each entry.

CodeAttribute attribute = ...;
StackMapTable table = (StackMapTable) attribute.getAttribute(StackMapTable.tag);
MyStackMapTableWalker walker = new MyStackMapTableWalker(table);
walker.parse();

title: How to loop through Javassist StackMapTable?
body:
Referring to this question, I still do not get the idea of looping through StackMapTable although I am able to get the Walker

StackMapTable stackMapTable = (StackMapTable) instructionToVisit.toCodeAttribute().getAttribute(StackMapTable.tag);

StackMapTable.Walker walker = new StackMapTable.Walker(stackMapTable);

Since integrating Javassist into the project, I am assuming that StackMapTablehttps://www.javassist.org/html/javassist/bytecode/StackMapTable.html is equivalent to StackDependencies

But I am not sure, is it able to be written like the for-loop block in this class

I tried to understand how to use Javassist StackMapTable to loop through dependencies in the Stack memory of Java. Besides that, I was hoping to get a validation whether my assumption on replacing Javassist StackMapTable to the existing Whyline StackDependencies.
